mappings.rationaleAI: Phoenicia's Levantine city-states (Tyre, Sidon, Byblos), seafaring trade networks, alphabet invention, and Carthaginian colonization directly map to F.2.a (Canaanite-Phoenician city-states c. 1100–700 BC), E.2.b.i (Levantine trade and the linear alphabet), I.5.a (Phoenician foundation of Carthage c. 814 BC), and I.5.b (Carthage's rise as Mediterranean power).

Alexander's conquest of the Achaemenid Persian Empire (330 BC) and the transition to Seleucid rule directly addresses G.3.b (Xerxes' successors and conquest by Alexander) and G.3.d (Seleucid rule after Alexander).

The Achaemenid Empire is the central subject of outline section G.3 and its sub-paths, covering Cyrus through Alexander's conquest, administrative innovation (satrapies), Zoroastrianism, Persepolis, and Greek conflicts (G.3.a–G.3.c), as well as Cyrus's foundational role at G.2.d.ii.

Mesopotamia is the overarching subject of outline division B and E.3, covering Sumerian, Akkadian, Babylonian, and Assyrian civilizations, cuneiform, city-states, hydraulic engineering, and successive imperial phases from the Neolithic through c. 900 BC.

Ancient Egypt maps comprehensively to sections C (Predynastic through Middle Kingdom) and E.1 (New Kingdom, 18th–20th dynasties), and F.1.a (21st–25th dynasties in decline), covering pharaonic continuity, divine kingship, pyramids, trade, and cultural developments across all these periods.

Cyrus II founded the Achaemenid Empire by defeating the Medes, Lydians, and Neo-Babylonians, directly addressing G.2.d (Kingdom of the Medes and establishment of the Achaemenid Persian Empire), G.2.d.ii (Cyrus's territorial expansion), and is foundational to G.3.

Sumer directly addresses B.1 and its sub-paths covering the earliest civilization in the Tigris-Euphrates valley, cuneiform invention, Uruk and early city-states (Kish, Ur, Lagash), temple economies, and Sumerian culture through the Ur III period.
Hammurabi's establishment of the Old Babylonian Empire and his law code directly map to B.1.d (Old Babylonian Period) and B.1.d.iii (dynasty of Hammurabi, law, society, and literature).

The Bronze Age article covers the metallurgical phase relevant to D.1.a.iii (Chalcolithic and Early Bronze Age in Syria-Palestine), D.2.a.iii (Bronze Age Troy and Anatolia), D.2.b (Bronze Ages in Cyprus), D.2.c (early Aegean Bronze Age), and E.5 (Mycenaean Late Bronze Age civilization).

As a broad survey covering West Asia, Egypt, Nubia, Aksum, Greece, and Rome from writing's invention through late antiquity, this article spans all major divisions of Section 911 and provides historiographic and general context for path A.

The Iron Age article covers the archaeological phase defined by iron adoption beginning c. 1200 BC in Anatolia, directly corresponding to outline section F (era of new states and the Iron Age, c. 1050–700 BC), including the Assyrian, Phoenician, and Hebrew states of F.1 and F.2.
African history covers Nubian/Sudanic civilizations and the Kingdom of Kush (I.1, I.2), Aksumite Ethiopia (I.4), and the Berbers, Carthage, and North African prehistory (I.5.a–I.5.c).

The Stone Age article covers Paleolithic, Mesolithic, and Neolithic periods directly relevant to D.1.a.i (Paleolithic and Mesolithic in Syria-Palestine), D.1.a.ii (Pre-Pottery Neolithic), D.2.a.i (Neolithic farming communities in Anatolia), D.2.c.i (Paleolithic-Neolithic in Greece and Aegean), and B.1.a (Late Neolithic in Mesopotamia).

The Neolithic origins of farming and animal domestication in Sumer and the Levant are directly addressed in B.1.a (pre-urban periods in Mesopotamia), D.1.a.i (development of horticulture), and D.1.a.iii (agricultural developments in the Chalcolithic and Early Bronze Age).

Ancient Rome's absorption of Carthage in the Punic Wars and Roman penetration into North Africa are covered at I.5.b (Carthage vs. Rome) and I.5.c (Roman penetration into North Africa, Numidia, and Mauretania).
